q: What should I do with spending immediately after a layoff?
a: Immediately cut discretionary spending to 50% on day one of layoff to extend runway without lifestyle shock.

q: What should I do with my 401k after a layoff?
a: Roll your 401(k) to an IRA within 60 days of leaving; direct rollovers avoid the 20% withholding.

q: When should I file for unemployment insurance?
a: File for unemployment insurance immediately — processing takes 2-4 weeks and back pay is not guaranteed.

q: How do I know how long my savings will last?
a: The 4% rule: to estimate runway, divide total savings by (annual expenses × 25). Or divide savings by monthly burn for months of runway.

q: Can I use my HSA after a layoff?
a: HSA funds are yours to keep and can be used for COBRA premiums if you're unemployed.

q: Should I pay off debt or build an emergency fund first during a layoff?
a: Pause extra debt payments and preserve cash. Minimum payments only — liquidity is more important than interest savings during unemployment.

q: How much emergency fund should I have?
a: Aim for 3-6 months of essential expenses (rent, food, utilities, minimum debt payments). During active job search, target 6 months.

q: Can I withdraw from my 401k early without penalty after a layoff?
a: Generally no — early withdrawals (before 59½) incur a 10% penalty plus income tax. Consider a 72(t) SEPP plan or Roth conversion ladder instead.
